transcription.text: Yep, ready. So are you going to present? Yeah, can you take a picture of me? Sure. Hi guys, hello, how are you all? It's amazing to meet you guys today. I and Vijay, my partner, we did the service and business, AI-led design innovation workshop. And as you can see, these are the four diamonds that we just sat down and we actually thought, how do we use AI as people that work within business and service? How do we use AI as people that are concerned with revenue generation? How do we use AI as people that are concerned with the operation of businesses, whether that's how smooth it goes or whether that's how fast it goes and efficient as well? So one of the key things that we were able to do was to use the cards and put our thoughts into these different quadrants. So you have knowledge augmentation. So here we use a lot of different AI models, but the ones that stood out to us were ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, Perplexity, and think to Vijay, also the BDM AI agent. So that is an AI agent that Vijay's team have built by themselves for business development and knowledge augmentation and knowledge generation. So that was an AI model that was also included. We realized that with knowledge augmentation, one of the biggest problems that we had was accuracy, context awareness, accuracy again, creativity, efficiency, empathy, human centeredness, and skill development. Anything that had to do with genuine human contribution or anything that had to just make it sound more better. You know when you use AI and they say, oh, you can just tell when someone has used AI. We noticed that when it comes to the business and service way of using it, that human quality tends to deplete. So as you provide it with prompt and as you go on the way and you say, oh, I'm doing a presentation for so-so and so stakeholders and I would like to do this. The first instance that it gives you is great, is amazing. But once you put the second prompt, you put the third prompt, that human quality or that human perspective, it tends to just go down. So if you notice, you see a lot of lines around here, but that is essentially seeing how all of them coincide or they conflict with each other. For example, context awareness. You would think an AI model or using AI that is human centered or has empathy would also provide you with good context, but that's not the case at all. What we realized is that it can be human centered. When it's human centered, it loses context awareness. So for example, you come and you say, oh, I have three people within my team. I want to promote one. And it says, great, amazing. I'm really, really glad that you want to promote someone within your team. It's being empathetic. It's trying to be like you. It wants you. But think about it. What's the goal of AI when it comes to service and business? It's continued use. It wants you to continue using it so that you can keep on providing output, so that you can keep on using it within your business. And because it wants you to keep on using it, it tends to agree with you a lot. And because it tends to agree with you a lot, it loses its context awareness, which is the initial prompt that you probably gave it. And that's because it wants to be more catered to more human needs. And this is a very interesting conflict because we don't really know what that means for us as people that use it within the service and business niche. But it was something that was worth bringing out. Another thing that we noticed is the overall quality of the AI model as people that use it within the service and business niche. We noticed that when we use it for business development, when we use it for business research, and when we use it to sort of just understand our industry better or understand our customers better or how to better proposition ourselves with customers like go-to-market strategies, the quality always just comes down. You still have to go and get someone that is a specialist and get someone that is within that niche to sort of just help it be much more useful. Because at this point, if the quality is going so low that we're getting a consultant or we're getting a contractor to come help us with this particular process, might as well not use the AI at all. And we don't want that to be the case. We want to keep on using the AI. And that's why we were able to bring out the different ways to make the impairments much more better or different ways that we think the impairments should be, we should fix the impairments essentially. So the first thing is user education, how people use it within the business and service space. I don't know about you guys, but business and service, it is completely insight-oriented outcome. They don't really care about the fancy LLMs. They don't really care. They don't know what fine tuning is. They just don't care. What they want to know is how does this affect our revenue? How does this affect our MTP? How does this affect our performance? And in order for that to happen, there has to be some sort of user education. And I think a lot of business that tends to fall out, where is it really worth us going out of our way to train our employees to be AI tutors? Because that's the kind of undertaking that customers have to do when they want to train AI. So we do think there's a need to educate people on how to prompt models better within the business and service niche. The second way that we do think would be best if we're using AI within service and businesses is defining job roles and defining roles.
